content.full_text:
Fern Pollrtcel PRACTICEs C ouvtlsSIoN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
September 1,2006
Robert Litzinger
724Kelsey Court
Santa Maria, California 93454
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-06-164
Dear Mr. Litzinger:
This letter is in response to your request regarding the conflict of interest
disclosure provisions of thi Political Reform Act (the "Act").r
QUESTION
Which payments that you receive from individuals for performing church-related
services are required to be reported on your statement of economic interests?
CONCLUSION
Payments you receive for conducting church-related services, such as weddings
and funerals, are either gifts or income, depending upon whether you provide equal or
greater value for the payments received. If you provide equal or greater value for the
payments, they are considered income and, if any one source provides you $500 or more
in a calendar year, you must report the source of the income on your statement of
economic interests. However, if you receive any gifts totaling $50 or more from a single
source in a calendar year, you must report the source of the grft(s). In addition, you are
subject to a gift limit of $360 per source received during a calendar year. These
payments are not considered prohibited honoraria.
FACTS
You have been nominated to the Block Grant Advisory Committee for the City of
Santa Maria. This position requires you to complete a statement of economic interests
(Form 700) and is designated as having full disclosure.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
c;:;'


File No. A-06-164
Page No. 2
You are a pastor and in the course of your occupation you receive "gifts and
support." Some of the gifts are from familymembers and some from local individuals
The gifts and support are for activities such as Pastor's Appreciation Sunday, and
weddings and funerals. You report all of this income to the IRS.
In a telephone conversation of August 25,2006, you stated that you are an
employee of the church, which is incorporated. You serve as the Chief Executive Officer
of the corporation. When you receive payments for Pastor's Appreciation Sunday,
weddings and funerals, and similar services, you personally retain these payments; they
are not glven to the corporation.
AiYALYSIS
As a public official holding a position that has "full disclosure," you are required
to report all investments, interests in real property and business positions held on the date
of assuming office, and income (including grfts) received during the 12 months prior to
the date of assuming office. (Regulation 18730(b)(6)(B).) Thereafter, you will be
required to file an annual statement of economic interests (Form 700) each year that you
continue to serve. When you leave yourposition, you will be required to file a leaving
office statement of economic interests. Nevertheless, there are several exceptions to the
requirement to report income and gifts.
Income
Section 82030 defines "income," in pertinent part, as:
"... a payrnent received, including but not limited to any salary, wage,
advance .... 'Income,' other than agift, does not include income received
from any source outside the jurisdiction and not doing business within the
jurisdiction, not planning to do business within the jurisdiction, or not
having done business within the jurisdiction during the two years prior to
the time any statement or other action is required .. ..,,
Therefore, income, other than gifts, received from sources outside the City of
Santa Maria is not reportable. Assuming your corporation is located in Santa }y'rana, any
salary, wage, or advance from the corporation is reportable as income on Schedule C of
Form 700. In addition, other income you receive, such as for Pastor's Appreciation
Sunday, weddings and funerals for which you provide equal or greater value, is also
reportable on Schedule C of Form 700.


File No. A-06-164
Page No. 3
Gifts
A glft is defined in section 82028, in pertinent part, as:
"... any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received ...."
Under section 89503(c) of the Act, designated employees of local govemment
agencies are subject to a limit on gifts thatmay be accepted in any calendar year. That
limit is adjusted biennially to account for inflation and is curently set at $360.
(Regulation 18940.2.) No designated employee of a local govemment agency may
accept a 91ft from any single source if the total value of gifts from that source will exceed
$360in acalendaryear.
If you provide "equal or greater value" for payments received for conducting
church services, and officiating at weddings and funerals, the payments would not bL
gifts subject to reporting and the $360limit.
In addition, under Commission regulation 18942, certain gifts are not considered
gifts under the Act and, therefore, are excluded from the gift limit. Such gifts are also not
required to be reported by the designated employee. Among the various exceptions in
regulation 18942 are gifts from a familymember. (See the instructions for Schedule D of
Form 700 for an itemized listing of family members covered by this exception.) Another
exception includes gifts exchanged between a designated employee and any other
individual on holidays, birthdays, and similar occasions, which are not substantially
disproportionate in value, are not gifts under the Act and need not be reported.
Accordingly, if you and members of your congregation, or anyone else, exchange
gifts on holidays, birthdays, and anniversaries, and if the gifts exchanged are not
sutstantially disproportionate in value, such gifts need not be reported on your statement
of economic interests. Otherwise, if you receive gifts from a single.orr."" totaling $50 or
more in a calendar year that do not meet an exception, you are required to report the
name and address, and a general description of the business activity, if any, of each
source on Schedule D. (Section87207.)
Honoraria
Under section 89502(c),a designated employee of a local govemment agency is
prohibited from receiving an honorarium from any reportable source of income or grfts.
An honorarium is defined as a payment for any speech glven, article published, or
attendance at arry public or private conference, convention, or meeting; however,
honoraria do not include pa5rment earned for personal services providid in connection
with an individual's business or bona fide trade or profession. (Section 89501(b)(l);
regulation 18932.1.)


File No. A-06-164
Page No. 4
In the Boatwrighr Advice Letter, No. A-93-323,this Commission previously
advised that an ordained minister hired as a legislative staff memb er may continue to
receive payment for conducting church services and continuing his ministry because
those services constitute the practice of or employment in, a bona fide business under the
Act. Accordingly the payments you receive for conducting church activities and
speaking as a licensed minister at weddings and funerals would not be prohibited
honoraria. Such payrnents would, however, constitute income and, as such, must be
reported on your statement of economic interests.
If you have other questions on this matter,please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
{n r, ,* ..! )'},( ,* ".*
By: Kevin S. Moen, PhD Otei
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

QUESTION: Which payments that you receive from individuals for performing church-related
services are required to be reported on your statement of economic interests?

CONCLUSION: Payments you receive for conducting church-related services, such as weddings
and funerals, are either gifts or income, depending upon whether you provide equal or
greater value for the payments received. If you provide equal or greater value for the
payments, they are considered income and, if any one source provides you $500 or more
in a calendar year, you must report the source of the income on your statement of
economic interests. However, if you receive any gifts totaling $50 or more from a single
source in a calendar year, you must report the source of the grft(s). In addition, you are
subject to a gift limit of $360 per source received during a calendar year. These
payments are not considered prohibited honoraria.
